dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 27-year-old female with rapid heart rate and palpitations; location is a clinic in Richmond. Call type is Heart Problems/AICD.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today, ma'am?
I'm having a racing heart and palpitations. It's been going on for about 20 minutes now.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where we are today?
My name is Lisa. We are in the clinic on the 5th floor in Richmond.
What is today's date for you, ma'am?
It's the 23rd of October, 2019, I believe.
Your sounds and words are clear.
Let's assess your breathing. Do you normally breathe this way or do you feel any difficulty?
Respiratory rate is 26. I'll note that down.
Your breathing is a bit fast but regular. Are you experiencing shortness of breath?
Blood pressure is 123 over 86, and heart rate is 179. These need immediate documentation.
Pulse is 179. That's very fast.
It's a rapid but regular rhythm.
Does the racing heart cause you any discomfort?
It is uncomfortable but not painful.
Let me go over your medical history. Do you have any conditions or known allergies?
I have Crohn's disease and am currently receiving an infusion at the clinic.
The patient states she has no known allergies, including environmental or drug.
SpO2 is 95% via pulse oximeter. I'll double-check for accuracy.
I'm unable to perform a glucose reading now.
Could you tell me what happened just before the symptoms started?
It started suddenly about 20 minutes ago while I was in the clinic. No apparent cause.
We're going to try a vagal maneuver. Can you blow into this syringe for several seconds?
We'll proceed with that first to see if we can slow your heart rate.
If that doesn't work, we'll start an IV in your left arm to administer medicine.
Per protocol 2-1, medical chest pain suspected. We'll follow it, starting with maneuvers and medication.
EKG shows SVT. Let me repeat it to monitor.
IV access established in your left arm. Administering 6 milligrams of Adenosine by rapid push now.
Heart rate has dropped to 106. Confirming a second time now.
Respiratory rate has decreased to 15.
Respiratory rate has decreased to 15.
Blood pressure is now 116 over 76. Still stable but improving.
SpO2 is now up to 98%. That's an improvement.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST and T-wave changes.
Another 12-lead shows sinus rhythm with short QT and some ST depression. We'll keep monitoring.
Given your symptoms and stable condition, we are transporting you to VCU Medical Center. Any issue with that location?
We'll assist you in walking to the stretcher and into the ambulance safely.
Just to confirm again, you mentioned earlier you don't have your ID. What is your full legal name and address?
My name is Lisa Thompson. I live at 997 Elmhurst Blvd, Apt 5.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time and situation-an A&Ox4 with a GCS of 15.
You've been assisted into the ambulance with the IV secured and everything stable.
Reading on SpO2 is 0% now, but sinus rhythm appears stable.
Heart rate is 92 now with regular rhythm. Still trending down.
Blood pressure is 122 over 79. That's definitely an improvement.
Lisa, any new symptoms at this time?
I feel much better. No new issues at all, thank you.
Are you still experiencing any discomfort or pain?
No pain, just the initial discomfort that has passed.
Respiratory rate is now 22. I'll keep checking it regularly.
Still unable to complete a glucose reading.
VCU has been informed. The patient is stable, HR is at 92, SpO2 at 0%, no chest pain.
All documentation and medical records are complete and ready for transfer.
We've arrived at VCU. Hospital staff will be meeting you now.
Care has been transferred. Patient is stable and improving. All systems addressed as per protocol.
